text.title: Feature Flag Rollout and Targeting Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the feature-flags-rollout-targeting methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Bucketing uses hash(flag_name+user_id)%100. Same user sees consistent variant across requests and sessions.

rationale.source: Kohavi et al — Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments

rule.rationale: Random per-request bucketing flips users in/out mid-flow; non-deterministic bucketing breaks A/B attribution.

rule.statement: Rollout percent moves only upward by default; flipping down is a rollback action, not a ramp step.
rule.rationale: Ramping down churns the cohort and re-exposes users to the control variant, contaminating measurement.

rule.statement: Apply targeting first (country/plan/role); rollout_percent applies only within targeted cohort.
rule.rationale: Percent within a targeted cohort is the only stable semantics; otherwise the actual reach is unknowable.

rule.statement: Use a stable user_id (account id, persistent device id). Never use IP or session id for rollout bucketing.
rule.rationale: Session/IP changes between visits; the user flips variants every session.

rule.statement: Ramp follows 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with at least one observation window between steps.
rule.rationale: Each step exposes a larger cohort; observation window catches guardrail regressions before next step.

rule.statement: For anonymous users (no user_id), either disable the flag or bucket by a stable device id; never default to True.
rule.rationale: Anonymous-True silently leaks new behavior to crawlers and scrapers.

rule.statement: If the flag is a binary kill-switch, traffic is fully anonymous with no stable id, or a managed SDK already buckets, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Bucketing logic only helps gradual / targeted rollout flags.
